subject: science
title: Cells — the building blocks
subtitle: What every living thing is made of

sections.heading: What is a cell?

predict.question: Before you read: what's the smallest part of a living thing that's still alive on its own?
predict.answer: A cell. It's the smallest unit that can take in food, grow, and copy itself. Anything smaller (like an organelle or a molecule) only works AS PART OF a cell.

sections.body:
Every living thing is made of cells. You are made of trillions. A cell is the smallest unit that can do all the jobs of being alive — taking in food, getting rid of waste, growing, and copying itself.

There are two big families: animal cells and plant cells. They share a lot but plant cells have extra parts because plants make their own food.

quickfact.label: typical animal cell width — ~50 fit across a dot

sections.heading: The main parts (organelles)
list: Nucleus — the control centre. Holds the DNA instructions.
Cytoplasm — the jelly inside, where chemical reactions happen.
Cell membrane — the skin that decides what gets in and out.
Mitochondria — release energy from food (in animal AND plant cells).
Cell wall — only in plant cells. Stiff outer layer for shape and support.
Chloroplasts — only in plant cells. Green dots that catch sunlight to make food (photosynthesis).
Vacuole — only in plant cells (large one). A bag of water that keeps the plant rigid.

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: Animal cells = NCM (Nucleus, Cytoplasm, Membrane). Plant cells add WCV (Wall, Chloroplasts, Vacuole).

example.title: What only plants have (and why)
steps: Cell wall — gives the plant its rigid shape (animals have a skeleton instead).
Chloroplasts — catch sunlight to make food (animals eat food, no need).
Big vacuole — stores water and keeps the plant firm (animals are mostly soft tissue).

why_prompt.question: Why don't animal cells need chloroplasts?
why_prompt.answer: Animals eat food that already has energy locked in it (plants, or other animals that ate plants). Plants can't move to find food, so they make their own from sunlight — that's what chloroplasts do.

sections.heading: How small are they?
sections.body: Most cells are too small to see without a microscope. A typical animal cell is about 0.02 mm wide — you'd fit fifty of them across the dot above this 'i'.

teach_back.prompt: Explain animal vs plant cells to a 7-year-old in 2 sentences.
checklist: Said which one has a cell wall
Mentioned chloroplasts for plants
Used simple words (no jargon)
teach_back.model_answer: Plant cells have a stiff outer wall and little green bits called chloroplasts that grab sunlight to make food. Animal cells are softer and can't make their own food, so animals have to eat.

tips: Easy memory: animal cells = NCM (Nucleus, Cytoplasm, Membrane). Plant cells add WCV (Wall, Chloroplasts, Vacuole).
Plants are green because chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, which reflects green light.
Mitochondria are sometimes called the cell's 'power plants' — they release energy from glucose.

subject: science
title: Particles & states of matter
subtitle: Why solids, liquids and gases behave differently

sections.heading: Everything is made of particles
sections.body: Every substance — your desk, your drink, the air — is made of tiny particles you can't see. The way those particles are arranged decides whether the substance is a solid, a liquid or a gas.

sections.heading: The three states
list: Solid — particles packed tight in a regular pattern. They vibrate but can't move past each other. Fixed shape, fixed volume.
Liquid — particles still touching but can slide past each other. Takes the shape of its container, fixed volume.
Gas — particles spread far apart, moving fast in all directions. Fills any container, expands to fit.

callout.title: Water is weird
callout.body: Most substances are denser as solids than as liquids. Water is the opposite — ice is LESS dense than liquid water, which is why ice floats. This matters for life: in winter, ice floats on top of lakes and insulates the water below, keeping fish alive.

sections.heading: Heating and cooling
sections.body:
Add heat → particles get more energy → they move more. Solid melts to liquid. Liquid evaporates (or boils) to gas. Cooling reverses each step: gas condenses to liquid, liquid freezes to solid.

The particles themselves don't change. Only their arrangement and how much they move.

example.title: What happens when ice melts
example.intro: Step through what's happening to the water particles:
steps: Ice particles are locked in a regular pattern, vibrating in place.
Heat is added → particles vibrate harder.
At 0°C, particles have enough energy to break free of their fixed positions — the ice melts.
Particles can now slide past each other → liquid water.
example.outro: Add more heat and you'll reach 100°C — particles break free of each other entirely → steam (gas).

quickfact.label: boiling point of water at sea level

teach_back.prompt: Explain why ice floats on water to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Mentioned ice is less dense than water
Used the word 'particles'
Kept it under 30 seconds
teach_back.model_answer: When water freezes into ice, the particles lock into a pattern that's actually more spaced out than liquid water. Same particles, bigger space — that makes ice lighter (less dense), so it floats on top.

tips: Water is an unusual exception: ice (solid) is LESS dense than water (liquid). That's why ice floats.
Gases are mostly empty space — that's why they squish so easily under pressure.
When you smell something, gas particles from that thing have travelled through the air to your nose.

subject: science
title: The Universe — Sun, Earth, Moon
subtitle: How our corner of space works

sections.heading: The Solar System
sections.body: Our Sun is a star. Around it orbit eight planets, lots of moons, asteroids and comets. The order of the planets, closest to the Sun first: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.

quickfact.label: average distance from the Earth to the Sun (1 AU)

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: "My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Noodles" — Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.

sections.heading: Why we have day and night
sections.body: Earth spins on its own axis once every 24 hours. The side facing the Sun has day. The side facing away has night. The Sun isn't moving across the sky — WE are moving.

callout.title: Common misconception
callout.body: The Sun does NOT 'rise' and 'set'. The horizon rotates away from the Sun (sunset) or towards it (sunrise) as Earth spins.

sections.heading: Why we have seasons
sections.body: Earth's axis is tilted at about 23.5°. As Earth orbits the Sun once a year, different hemispheres point towards the Sun at different times. When it's tilted towards us → longer days, more direct sunlight → summer. Tilted away → winter.

example.title: June in the UK vs December in the UK
steps: June: northern hemisphere tilts towards the Sun.
Sun is high in the sky, daylight is long (~16+ hours).
Sunlight hits the ground steeply → concentrated heat → summer.
December: same Earth, opposite point in orbit. North tilts away.
Sun is low, days are short (~8 hours), light hits at a shallow angle → spread thin → winter.
example.outro: Note: it's NOT because Earth is closer or further from the Sun — that barely changes.

sections.heading: Phases of the Moon
sections.body: The Sun always lights up half the Moon. As the Moon orbits Earth (about every 27 days), we see different proportions of the lit half. New Moon (lit half facing away) → crescent → half → full Moon (lit half facing us) → and back again.

quickfact.label: time for the Moon to orbit Earth once

teach_back.prompt: Explain why we have day and night to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Said the Earth spins
Mentioned the Sun stays still (from our view)
Didn't say 'the Sun goes round the Earth'
teach_back.model_answer: The Earth spins like a slow top — once every 24 hours. When your side faces the Sun it's day; when your side faces away it's night. The Sun isn't moving across the sky — we are.

tips: Mnemonic for planet order: "My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Noodles".
Pluto isn't on the list — it was reclassified as a 'dwarf planet' in 2006.
Seasons are about TILT, not distance. Earth is actually closest to the Sun in January.

subject: maths
title: Triangles, polygons & shape names
subtitle: Naming shapes by their sides and angles

sections.heading: Triangles by their sides
list: Equilateral — all 3 sides equal, all 3 angles equal (each 60°).
Isosceles — exactly 2 sides equal. The two angles opposite those equal sides are also equal.
Scalene — all 3 sides different, all 3 angles different.
Right-angled — one angle is exactly 90° (a right angle).

callout.title: Quick spot
callout.body: Equal sides ↔ equal opposite angles. Always. So if a triangle has two equal sides, the angles opposite them must match.

sections.heading: The angle rule
sections.body: The three angles inside ANY triangle add up to 180°. So if you know two angles, you can always work out the third by subtracting them from 180°.

example.title: Find the missing angle
example.intro: A triangle has angles of 70° and 55°. Find the third:
steps: Add the known angles: 70 + 55 = 125°.
Subtract from 180°: 180 − 125 = 55°.
The missing angle is 55°.
example.outro: Notice: two angles are 55° → this is also an isosceles triangle.

quickfact.label: angles in any triangle add to this

sections.heading: Polygons by side count
list: 3 sides — triangle
4 sides — quadrilateral (square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite are all quadrilaterals)
5 sides — pentagon
6 sides — hexagon
7 sides — heptagon
8 sides — octagon
10 sides — decagon

callout.title: Greek prefix tip
callout.body: Penta = 5, hexa = 6, octa = 8 (think octopus, eight legs), deca = 10 (decade = 10 years).

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a 7-year-old how to tell a hexagon from an octagon.
checklist: Said to count the sides
Hexagon = 6, octagon = 8
Linked octagon to 'octopus = 8 legs'
teach_back.model_answer: Count the sides. A hexagon has 6 sides (think honeycomb). An octagon has 8 sides (think OCTopus — 8 legs).

tips: A 'regular' polygon means all sides equal AND all angles equal. A regular triangle = equilateral. A regular quadrilateral = a square.
Quick check: angles in a triangle ALWAYS = 180°. Angles in a quadrilateral always = 360°.
Sum of interior angles in any polygon = (n − 2) × 180°, where n = number of sides.

subject: maths
title: Square & cube numbers
subtitle: Multiplying a number by itself

sections.heading: Square numbers
sections.body:
A square number is what you get when you multiply a whole number by itself.

1² = 1 × 1 = 1
2² = 2 × 2 = 4
3² = 3 × 3 = 9
4² = 4 × 4 = 16
5² = 5 × 5 = 25

The little 2 means "squared" — you literally make a square that's that wide and that tall.

callout.title: Why "squared"?
callout.body: Because 5² is the area of a real 5-by-5 square: 25 little unit squares. The diagram above shows 1², 2², 3², 4² as actual squares.

sections.heading: Cube numbers
sections.body:
A cube number is what you get when you multiply a whole number by itself THREE times. The little 3 means "cubed".

1³ = 1 × 1 × 1 = 1
2³ = 2 × 2 × 2 = 8
3³ = 3 × 3 × 3 = 27
4³ = 4 × 4 × 4 = 64
5³ = 5 × 5 × 5 = 125

sections.heading: Square root
sections.body: The square root is the reverse of squaring. The square root of 49 is 7, because 7 × 7 = 49. The symbol is √. So √49 = 7.

example.title: Work out √144
steps: Ask: what number times itself equals 144?
Try 10: 10 × 10 = 100 (too small).
example.outro: Memorising 1² to 12² makes square roots up to 144 instant.

teach_back.prompt: Explain what 5² means to a 7-year-old in 2 sentences.
checklist: Said it's 5 × 5
Mentioned the answer is 25
Used 'a square' as a visual
teach_back.model_answer: 5² means 5 times 5. Imagine making a square that's 5 wide and 5 tall — you'd need 25 little tiles to fill it, and 25 is the answer.

tips: Memorise the squares from 1² to 12² — they come up in tons of questions.
If you square a negative number, you get a POSITIVE answer (negative × negative = positive).

subject: english
title: Word classes — the parts of speech
subtitle: What each word in a sentence is doing

sections.heading: The main word classes
list: Noun — a person, place, thing or idea. (cat, London, freedom)
Verb — a doing or being word. (run, is, think)
Adjective — describes a noun. (happy, blue, ancient)
Adverb — describes a verb (or an adjective). Often ends in -ly. (quickly, very, well)
Pronoun — stands in for a noun. (he, she, it, they, we)
Preposition — shows position or direction. (under, above, between, into)
Conjunction — joins words or sentences. (and, but, because, although)
Determiner — comes before a noun, points it out. (the, a, this, my, some)

callout.title: The cleanest split
callout.body: Adjectives describe NOUNS. Adverbs describe VERBS (or other adjectives). Mixing these up is the #1 wrong-class error.

sections.heading: How to spot them in a sentence

example.title: Break down: "The huge dog barked loudly under the table."
steps: The → determiner (points out which dog)
huge → adjective (describes 'dog')
dog → noun (the thing)
barked → verb (the action)
loudly → adverb (describes how it barked)
under → preposition (position)
the → determiner
table → noun

sections.heading: Quick tests you can run
sections.body:
If you can put 'a' or 'the' in front of a word and it makes sense, it's almost certainly a noun (a cat, the freedom).

If the word ends in -ly and adds info to a verb, it's an adverb (quickly, slowly, happily).

If the word can be replaced with 'is/was/runs/jumps' and the sentence still makes sense, that slot wants a verb.

callout.title: Watch out
callout.body: Some words swap class depending on use. "Run" is a verb in "I run" but a noun in "a long run". Always check what it's DOING in the sentence.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.body: Examiners ask you to identify word classes because they want to see you can analyse HOW a sentence works. Once you can spot a verb, an adjective, a noun cleanly, you can talk about the writer's choices: "the verb 'crept' makes the cat seem nervous".

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a 7-year-old what a verb is.
checklist: Called it a 'doing word'
Gave at least one example
Didn't use the word 'predicate'
teach_back.model_answer: A verb is a doing word — it tells you what someone is DOING in a sentence. 'Run', 'jump', 'eat', 'sleep', 'is' — they're all verbs.

tips: Adverbs often end in -ly — but not always (well, fast, often, very).
If you can swap a word with another known one of the same class without breaking the sentence, you've identified it.
Pronouns are noun replacements. Spot them by asking: who or what could this be referring to?

subject: english
title: Similes, metaphors & co.
subtitle: How writers paint with words

sections.heading: Simile
sections.body:
A simile compares two things using the words 'like' or 'as'.

• "Her hair shone like the sun."
• "He was as quiet as a mouse."

The LIKE or AS is the giveaway.

callout.title: Why writers use it
callout.body: Similes paint a picture quickly. "He ran fast" tells. "He ran like a startled deer" shows you the panic AND the speed in one phrase.

sections.heading: Metaphor
sections.body:
A metaphor says something IS something else. It's a stronger comparison — no 'like' or 'as'.

• "The classroom was a zoo." (it WAS a zoo, not LIKE a zoo)
• "He's a rock in a crisis."

callout.title: Stronger than simile
callout.body: A metaphor commits — it claims the two things are the same. That makes the image more vivid. "Her eyes were diamonds" hits harder than "like diamonds".

sections.heading: Personification
sections.body:
Giving a non-human thing human qualities or actions.

• "The wind whispered through the trees."
• "Time crawled."

Wind doesn't actually whisper. Time doesn't actually crawl. But the writer makes them feel alive.

example.title: Spot the technique
example.intro: "The angry sea hurled itself against the cliffs."
steps: What's being described? The sea.
What human action? Being angry, hurling — both human emotions/movements.
Technique: personification — sea isn't actually angry, but the writer wants us to feel its violence.

sections.heading: Alliteration & onomatopoeia
list: Alliteration — same starting sound repeated. "Slithering, slimy snakes."
Onomatopoeia — a word that sounds like the thing it describes. "Bang!" "Crash!" "Splat!"

quickfact.label: main techniques to know — simile, metaphor, personification, alliteration, onomatopoeia

teach_back.prompt: Explain the difference between a simile and a metaphor to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Said simile uses 'like' or 'as'
Said metaphor says it IS the thing
Gave one example of each
teach_back.model_answer: A simile says one thing is LIKE another — 'her hair was like sunshine'. A metaphor says one thing IS another — 'her hair was sunshine'. The metaphor commits, which makes the picture stronger.

tips: Simile = LIKE / AS. Metaphor = IS / WAS. That's the cleanest split.
Personification almost always uses a verb a human would do (whispered, danced, laughed).
When you spot any of these, ask: 'What effect does it have? What picture does it put in my head?' That's the analysis bit examiners want.

subject: history
title: The Norman Conquest
subtitle: 1066 — the year everything changed

sections.heading: The story
sections.body:
When King Edward the Confessor died in early 1066, three different men thought they should be the next King of England. Harold Godwinson (an English earl) was crowned almost immediately. But Harald Hardrada (a Viking) and William of Normandy (a Frenchman) both invaded.

Harold beat Hardrada at Stamford Bridge in September 1066 — then had to march south because William had landed on the south coast. Harold's exhausted army met William at the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066. Harold was killed (the Bayeux Tapestry shows an arrow striking his eye). William won. He was crowned King of England on Christmas Day 1066.

quickfact.label: the year of the Battle of Hastings

callout.title: Date to nail
callout.body: 14 October 1066 — Battle of Hastings. Memorise this one date and a lot falls into place.

sections.heading: What changed afterwards
sections.body:
William didn't just win one battle — he changed England. He replaced almost every English noble with a Norman one. He built castles (mostly motte-and-bailey, made of earth and timber) all over the country to hold down the population.

He ordered the Domesday Book in 1086 — a massive survey recording who owned what, so he could tax it properly. He brought Norman French as the language of the court — that's why English now has both Saxon words like 'cow' and French-origin words like 'beef'.

example.title: Why William's victory stuck
example.intro: It wasn't just winning one battle — three things kept the Normans in power:
steps: Replace the rulers — almost every English noble was swapped for a Norman one within 5 years.
Build castles — over 500 motte-and-bailey castles thrown up across England by 1086, controlling local areas.
Count everything — the Domesday Book (1086) recorded who owned what, so William could tax it all.
example.outro: By the time William died in 1087, England's ruling class, language and landscape were Norman.

callout.title: Common confusion
callout.body: Two men called Harold/Harald in 1066. Harold Godwinson is the English king who lost at Hastings. Harald Hardrada is the Viking king who lost earlier at Stamford Bridge. Both lost. William won.

sections.heading: The feudal system
sections.body:
Society was organised as a pyramid:

• King (William) at the top — owned all the land in theory
• Barons — held big chunks from the King in return for loyalty + soldiers
• Knights — held smaller chunks from the Barons in return for fighting
• Peasants — at the bottom — worked the land

This is what the diagram above shows.

teach_back.prompt: Explain why 1066 matters to a 7-year-old in 2 sentences.
checklist: Mentioned William or the Normans winning
Said England changed afterwards
Didn't try to list every detail
teach_back.model_answer: In 1066 a French duke called William invaded England, won a big battle at Hastings, and made himself king. He swapped almost every English noble for a French one — that's why English now has loads of French-origin words like 'beef' and 'castle'.

tips: Date to know cold: 14 October 1066, Battle of Hastings.
Don't confuse Harold (English, 1066) with Harald (Viking, also 1066). Both lost. William won.
The Bayeux Tapestry is a 70-metre embroidery telling the Norman version of events. It's the closest thing to a contemporary news report from 1066.

subject: geography
title: Reading a map — symbols, scale, contours
subtitle: Turning a flat map into a 3D understanding

sections.heading: Map symbols
sections.body:
An OS (Ordnance Survey) map uses tiny pictures called symbols to represent things on the ground — a church, a campsite, a footpath, woodland. The map's KEY (or legend) explains what each symbol means.

Always check the key first. Symbols are standard: a blue triangle for a campsite, a black cross for a church without a tower, a brown dotted line for a footpath, etc.

callout.title: First move on any map
callout.body: Find the KEY (usually bottom-left). Two minutes spent reading it saves ten minutes of guesswork later.

sections.heading: Scale
sections.body:
Scale tells you how distance on the map matches distance in real life. Common UK map scales:

• 1 : 25,000 — 1 cm on the map = 25,000 cm = 250 m in real life. So 4 cm = 1 km.
• 1 : 50,000 — 1 cm on the map = 500 m in real life. So 2 cm = 1 km.

To find a real distance: measure on the map, then multiply by the scale.

example.title: Worked example
example.intro: On a 1 : 25,000 map, a footpath measures 6 cm. How long is it in real life?
steps: Multiply: 6 cm × 25,000 = 150,000 cm.
Convert to metres: 150,000 ÷ 100 = 1,500 m.
Convert to km: 1,500 ÷ 1,000 = 1.5 km.
Answer: the footpath is 1.5 km long.

sections.heading: Grid references
sections.body:
OS maps have a grid of squares (each 1 km × 1 km). To pinpoint a place:

• Four-figure grid reference (e.g. 2438) — gives you the bottom-left corner of one 1 km square. Read along the bottom (eastings) first, then up the side (northings).
• Six-figure grid reference (e.g. 245384) — divides each 1 km square into a 10 × 10 grid, pinpointing a 100 m square.

callout.title: Memory rule
callout.body: "Along the corridor, then up the stairs." Eastings first (the bottom-of-the-page numbers), then northings (the side-of-the-page numbers).

quickfact.label: the square pinpointed by a 6-figure grid reference

sections.heading: Contour lines
sections.body:
Brown lines on an OS map joining points of equal height. They turn a flat map into 3D in your head:

• Lines close together = steep slope (the land rises sharply over a short distance).
• Lines far apart = gentle slope.
• Concentric rings = a hill (the smallest ring at the centre is the summit). The diagram above shows this.

The number on each contour tells you the height in metres above sea level.

teach_back.prompt: Explain how to read a 4-figure grid reference to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Mentioned 'along the corridor, up the stairs'
Said eastings come first
Gave a specific example
teach_back.model_answer: Read the first two numbers ALONG (left to right). Read the next two numbers UP (bottom to top). Always 'along the corridor, then up the stairs' — that order, every time. So 23 45 means: along to 23, then up to 45.

tips: Bearings are measured CLOCKWISE from north, in degrees, written as 3 digits. So east = 090°, south = 180°, west = 270°.
Closely spaced contours on a route mean a hard climb — useful when planning a walk.
Standard 8-point compass: N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW. Each is 45° apart.

subject: computing
title: E-safety basics
subtitle: Staying safe online — the things that actually matter

sections.heading: Personal information
sections.body:
Never share your real name (full), home address, school, phone number, or family details with strangers online. This includes in chat rooms, game lobbies, and social media DMs from people you don't know in real life.

If someone you've never met asks for any of this, the right move is always: don't reply, don't send anything, and tell a trusted adult (parent, teacher).

callout.title: Big red flag
callout.body: Anyone who pressures you for personal info, photos, or to keep something secret from your parents — that's a warning sign. Tell a trusted adult straight away.

sections.heading: Strong passwords
sections.body:
A strong password:

• Is at least 8 characters long
• Mixes UPPER and lower case letters
• Includes numbers and a symbol or two
• Isn't a real word, your name, or your birthday

"Tiger123" is weak. "p@5sW0rd!92" is strong. Better still: a memorable phrase like "3Blue!Cats#JumpHigh".

example.title: Weak vs strong
steps: ❌ password — top of every hacker's first-guess list.
❌ Aaryan2014 — your name + birth year is easily guessable.
❌ qwerty — keyboard pattern, instantly tried.
✓ p@5sW0rd!92 — mixed case, numbers, symbols, no real word.
✓ 3Blue!Cats#JumpHigh — a long passphrase, easy to remember, hard to crack.

quickfact.label: minimum characters in a strong password

sections.heading: Digital footprint
sections.body:
Everything you post, like, search and login to online leaves a trail — your 'digital footprint'. Screenshots happen. Even after you 'delete' something, someone may have already saved it.

The rule of thumb: don't post anything you wouldn't be comfortable showing your grandmother, or a future employer. Embarrassing photos can resurface years later.

callout.title: The grandma test
callout.body: Before you post or send: would you be okay with your grandma seeing it? If no, don't send.

sections.heading: Copyright
sections.body:
Anything original someone creates — a song, a video, a piece of writing, a photo — is automatically theirs by law. That's copyright. Using their work without permission can be illegal, even for a school project.

If you want music for a video, look for 'royalty-free' tracks (legal to use freely) or get the creator's permission.

teach_back.prompt: Explain SMART e-safety to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Decoded at least 3 of the SMART letters
Used 'don't' for at least one
Mentioned telling an adult
teach_back.model_answer: SMART means: Stay safe (don't share your address or phone number), don't Meet anyone you only know online, don't Accept random messages or files, check info is Reliable before believing it, and Tell a trusted grown-up if anything feels weird.

tips: Set social profiles to PRIVATE by default — only people you've accepted can see your posts.
If you're being bullied online: save evidence (screenshots), don't reply, tell a trusted adult, and use the platform's REPORT button. Replying often makes it worse.
If something online makes you uncomfortable, you don't have to figure out why — just close it and tell someone. Trust the feeling.

subject: computing
title: Computational thinking
subtitle: How programmers break problems apart

sections.heading: Decomposition
sections.body:
Big problems are scary. Computational thinking starts by breaking a problem into smaller, manageable pieces.

Example: "Make a game." Decompose into: design the player character; write the movement code; design the enemies; write the score logic; design the menu screen. Now each piece is doable on its own.

callout.title: Real-world parallel
callout.body: Cooking a 3-course meal? You don't try to do it all at once. You decompose: write the menu, write the shopping list, prep starters, cook mains, plate dessert. Same idea.

sections.heading: Pattern recognition
sections.body:
Look for similarities between the smaller pieces — and similarities to problems you've already solved. If two enemies move the same way, write the movement code ONCE and use it for both. If you've made a score counter before, you can reuse the same approach.

Spotting patterns saves time.

sections.heading: Abstraction
sections.body:
Strip away the unnecessary detail. Focus only on what matters for the problem.

A London Underground map is a brilliant example of abstraction. The actual track in real life is twisty and not to scale — but the map straightens it all out and just shows you which stations connect. That's abstraction: hide what you don't need.

example.title: Abstraction in action — building a school timetable
steps: Day of the week
Time of each lesson
Subject + room number
example.outro: What you DON'T need (strip out): teacher's hair colour, classroom paint colour, weather forecast.

sections.heading: Algorithms
sections.body: Once you've decomposed, spotted patterns, and abstracted away the noise, write a step-by-step set of instructions to solve the problem. That's an algorithm. A programmer then translates the algorithm into code.

teach_back.prompt: Explain decomposition to a 7-year-old.
checklist: Used the phrase 'break it down' or similar
Gave an everyday example
Didn't use jargon
teach_back.model_answer: Decomposition means breaking a big problem into small steps. Like getting dressed: you don't think 'get dressed' as one job — you think 'put on socks, put on shirt, put on trousers'. Computers solve problems the same way.

tips: Order to remember: DPAA — Decompose, Patterns, Abstract, Algorithm.
An algorithm is the recipe. Code is the recipe written in a language a computer understands.
Whenever you find yourself doing the same thing twice in code, that's a sign you can spot a pattern and simplify.

subject: french
title: Saying your name & age
subtitle: Year 7 French — the very first phrases

sections.heading: Saying your name
sections.body:
"Je m'appelle ___" = "My name is ___" (literally "I call myself").

If someone asks you "Comment tu t'appelles?" ("What's your name?"), reply "Je m'appelle Alex" (or whatever your name is).

The "m'" is a contraction of "me" — it shrinks because the next word starts with a vowel sound. French does this all the time.

callout.title: Pronunciation
callout.body: "Je m'appelle" sounds like "zhuh ma-PELL". The final 'e' on appelle is silent.

sections.heading: Saying your age
sections.body:
Important: in French you HAVE your age. Not "I AM twelve" but "I HAVE twelve years".

"J'ai douze ans" = "I am 12 years old" (literally "I have twelve years")

If someone asks "Quel âge as-tu?" ("How old are you?"), reply "J'ai ___ ans".

callout.title: Don't say "Je suis douze"
callout.body: That literally means "I am twelve" — but it doesn't mean 12 years old in French. Always use J'AI for age.

example.title: Mini dialogue
steps: A: Comment tu t'appelles?  → "What's your name?"
B: Je m'appelle Aaryan.  → "My name is Aaryan."
A: Quel âge as-tu?  → "How old are you?"
B: J'ai douze ans.  → "I am 12."

sections.heading: Numbers 1–20
list: 1 un · 2 deux · 3 trois · 4 quatre · 5 cinq
6 six · 7 sept · 8 huit · 9 neuf · 10 dix
11 onze · 12 douze · 13 treize · 14 quatorze · 15 quinze
16 seize · 17 dix-sept · 18 dix-huit · 19 dix-neuf · 20 vingt

sections.heading: Pleased to meet you
sections.body: After someone tells you their name, the polite response is "Enchanté" (boy speaking) or "Enchantée" (girl speaking) — "Pleased to meet you". They sound exactly the same out loud; only the spelling differs.

teach_back.prompt: Tell a 7-year-old how to ask someone's age in French and what's weird about the answer.
checklist: Used 'Quel âge as-tu?' or similar
Mentioned the verb avoir (to have)
Said you HAVE an age, not ARE one
teach_back.model_answer: Ask 'Quel âge as-tu?' — literally 'what age have you?' To answer, you say 'J'ai 12 ans' which means 'I have 12 years'. In French you HAVE an age, not ARE one — that's the classic trap.

tips: Use the LISTEN button on French questions to hear native pronunciation. Use SLOW for tricky bits.
"Je suis" = I AM. "J'ai" = I HAVE. Don't mix them up — age uses j'ai, not je suis.
Treize (13) and trente (30) sound similar but not the same. Treize ends with a soft 'z' sound; trente ends like 'tont'.

subject: science
title: Forces and motion
subtitle: Why things speed up, slow down, or stay still

sections.heading: What is a force?

predict.question: Before you read: name three things that can SPEED UP a moving object.
predict.answer: Pushing it (like kicking a football). Pulling it (towing a car). And gravity (a falling apple). All three are forces — pushes or pulls.

sections.body:
A force is a push or a pull. You feel forces every second: gravity pulling you down into your chair, friction stopping you sliding when you walk, the wind pushing you when you cycle.

Forces are measured in newtons (N) — named after Sir Isaac Newton. A medium apple weighs about 1 N. The pull of Earth's gravity on a 50 kg kid is about 500 N.

sections.heading: Newton's three laws — Year-7 short version
list: 1st law: a thing keeps doing what it's doing (still or moving the same way) UNLESS a force acts on it.
2nd law: bigger force = bigger acceleration. Heavier object = smaller acceleration for the same force. (F = m × a.)
3rd law: every push has an equal and opposite push back. You push the ground; the ground pushes you forward.

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: 1st: lazy. 2nd: maths. 3rd: pairs. If you can remember those three words, you can rebuild the laws.

sections.heading: Balanced vs unbalanced
sections.body:
When forces on an object are BALANCED (equal, opposite), the object stays still or keeps moving the same way. When they're UNBALANCED, the object speeds up, slows down, or changes direction.

A book on a table: gravity pulls it down, the table pushes it up — balanced, book sits still. Push the book — your push is unbalanced by friction → book accelerates briefly until friction matches, then stops.

example.title: A skydiver
example.intro: Force changes during a skydive show all of this in action:
steps: Just out of the plane: gravity pulls down (large force), air resistance is small. UNBALANCED → accelerates downward.
Faster fall: air resistance grows.
When air resistance equals gravity → balanced → constant speed (terminal velocity).
Parachute opens: air resistance suddenly huge, MORE than gravity. Net force points up → DECELERATES.
New balance at much slower speed → safe landing.

why_prompt.question: Why does the skydiver eventually fall at a constant speed?
why_prompt.answer: Because air resistance grows with speed. Once it's big enough to balance gravity, there's no NET force, so no more acceleration. The skydiver keeps falling at the speed where the two forces are equal — terminal velocity.

sections.heading: Friction — the force that resists motion
sections.body:
Friction is the force that opposes movement between two surfaces touching each other. Rough surfaces have lots of friction (rubber on tarmac); smooth ones have less (ice on ice). Without friction, your feet would slip every time you tried to walk.

Friction always acts AGAINST motion. Push a box right → friction points left.

callout.title: Useful AND annoying
callout.body: Friction lets you walk, brake, hold a pen. It also wears down trainers, slows down cars (so you need more fuel) and makes machines hot. Engineers spend a lot of time deciding when to add friction (brakes) and when to remove it (oiled bearings).

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a 7-year-old why you don't slip on a normal floor but you do slip on ice.
checklist: Used the word 'friction'
Said ice is smooth / floors are rough
Linked it to your feet not slipping
teach_back.model_answer: Walking works because of friction — a grippy force between your foot and the floor that stops your foot sliding. A normal floor is rough enough for lots of friction. Ice is super-smooth, so there's barely any friction, and your foot slides instead of pushing you forward.

tips: Force is measured in newtons (N). Weight is also a force, so it's in newtons — a 50 kg kid WEIGHS about 500 N.
If forces are balanced, motion DOESN'T change. If unbalanced, it does.
Friction always opposes motion. Air resistance is just friction with air.

subject: science
title: Energy — the 8 stores
subtitle: Where energy is held, and how it moves between things

sections.heading: Energy isn't 'made'

predict.question: Before you read: when a battery powers a torch, where does the light energy come from?
predict.answer: From the chemical store inside the battery. The battery doesn't 'make' light — it transfers chemical energy to the bulb, which turns it into light + heat. Energy is never created; only moved.

sections.body:
The most important rule in physics: energy is never created or destroyed, only TRANSFERRED from one store to another. This is called the conservation of energy.

When you eat a sandwich, chemical energy in the bread moves to chemical energy in your muscles, then to kinetic (movement) energy as you run, then to thermal (heat) energy as friction slows you down. Same energy. Different stores.

sections.heading: The 8 energy stores
list: Kinetic — anything that's moving (a running kid, a thrown ball)
Gravitational — anything raised up high (a book on a shelf, a kid on a slide)
Elastic — anything stretched or squashed (a bow, a spring, a trampoline)
Chemical — anything with energy locked in chemical bonds (food, fuel, batteries)
Nuclear — energy locked in the nucleus of atoms (uranium, the Sun)
Thermal — anything hotter than its surroundings (a kettle, a fire)
Magnetic — energy in magnetic fields (a magnet pulling iron)
Electrostatic — energy in static charges (rubbed balloon sticking to hair)

callout.title: Watch out — examiners want STORES, not 'types'
callout.body: Old textbooks say 'types of energy' (like 'sound energy', 'light energy'). The new spec says STORES — and 'sound' and 'light' aren't stores, they're ways energy MOVES (called pathways). Use the 8 names above, exactly.

sections.heading: Energy pathways — how energy MOVES
sections.body:
Energy moves between stores along PATHWAYS. There are four:
• Mechanical (forces) — pushing, pulling, lifting
• Electrical — current in a wire
• Heating — warmer thing → cooler thing
• Radiation — light, sound, infrared, etc.

example.title: A torch in action
example.intro: Trace the energy through the system:
steps: Start: chemical store (battery).
Pathway: electrical (current flows when you press the switch).
Bulb: chemical → thermal store (the filament gets hot).
Pathway: radiation (light leaves the bulb).
Some energy: thermal pathway too (the bulb feels warm — that's wasted energy).
example.outro: All the energy is still there — just split between useful (light) and wasted (heat in the bulb).

sections.heading: Useful vs wasted — efficiency
sections.body:
Real systems always waste some energy as heat. Efficiency = useful energy out ÷ total energy in (× 100%).

A modern LED bulb is about 90% efficient — almost all the electrical energy becomes light. An old-style filament bulb is about 5% — most of the energy goes to heat (which is why old bulbs got so hot).

quickfact.label: efficiency of a 1950s filament bulb (the rest = heat)

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a 7-year-old where the energy goes when you eat a banana and run around.
checklist: Said 'chemical store' for the banana
Said 'kinetic store' for the running
Mentioned heat (thermal) as the wasted bit
teach_back.model_answer: The banana has chemical energy locked in its sugars. Your stomach unlocks it and your muscles use it to move (kinetic energy). Some of it leaks out as heat (thermal energy) — that's why you feel warm after running. Same energy, different stores.

tips: Use STORE names exactly: kinetic, gravitational, elastic, chemical, nuclear, thermal, magnetic, electrostatic. Saying 'movement energy' instead of 'kinetic store' loses marks.
Energy is never destroyed. If it 'disappears' it's been transferred — usually to heat.
Pathways move energy between stores. The four are: mechanical, electrical, heating, radiation.

subject: science
title: Acids and alkalis
subtitle: The pH scale and what it actually measures

sections.heading: What is pH?

predict.question: Before you read: which has a lower pH — lemon juice or soap?
predict.answer: Lemon juice. Lower pH = more acidic. Lemon juice is around pH 2 (acidic); soap is around pH 9 (alkaline). Pure water sits in the middle at pH 7.

sections.body:
pH is a number from 0 to 14 that tells you how acidic or alkaline something is. The scale has THREE zones:
• 0–6: ACIDIC (lower = stronger acid)
• 7: NEUTRAL (pure water)
• 8–14: ALKALINE (higher = stronger alkali)

pH is measured with universal indicator (changes colour) or a pH meter (digital number).

sections.heading: Common examples
list: pH 1 — battery acid (very strong acid, dangerous)
pH 2 — lemon juice, stomach acid
pH 4 — orange juice, vinegar
pH 5 — coffee, milk
pH 7 — pure water (neutral)
pH 8 — sea water, baking soda
pH 9 — soap, washing-up liquid
pH 11 — household ammonia
pH 14 — drain cleaner (very strong alkali, dangerous)

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: Acid vs alkali: think 'A for Acid, A for Below 7'. The Cs (Caustic, Cleaning products) live above 7.

sections.heading: Neutralisation — opposites cancel
sections.body:
Mix an acid and an alkali → they react and cancel each other out. The product is a SALT plus WATER. The pH moves towards 7.

This is how indigestion tablets work: stomach acid (pH 2) is too strong → take a base like calcium carbonate → it neutralises the acid up to a comfortable pH 5–6.

example.title: Hydrochloric acid + sodium hydroxide
steps: Acid: HCl (hydrochloric acid, pH 1).
Alkali: NaOH (sodium hydroxide, pH 13).
Mix them in equal amounts: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.
Products: salt (sodium chloride — same as table salt) + water (pH 7).
The strong acid and strong alkali both disappeared. They neutralised.

why_prompt.question: Why do bee stings hurt less if you put bicarbonate of soda on them?
why_prompt.answer: A bee sting is acidic. Bicarbonate of soda is a weak alkali. The two NEUTRALISE each other, moving the pH near the sting back towards 7 — which stops the chemical reaction in your skin that causes the pain.

sections.heading: Indicators — colours that tell you pH
sections.body:
An indicator is a chemical that changes colour at different pHs. The most common at school is universal indicator:
• Red → strong acid
• Orange/Yellow → weak acid
• Green → neutral
• Blue → weak alkali
• Purple → strong alkali

Litmus paper is simpler — red in acid, blue in alkali, no colour gradient.

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a 7-year-old how pH works using lemon juice and soap.
checklist: Mentioned pH is a number from 0 to 14
Said acids are below 7 and alkalis above
Used lemon as acid + soap as alkali
teach_back.model_answer: pH is a number that says how acidic or alkaline something is. Lemon juice is sour and has pH 2 — that means strong acid. Soap is bitter and has pH 9 — that means alkali. Pure water sits at 7 in the middle, neither sour nor bitter.

tips: Lower pH = STRONGER acid (not weaker). pH 1 is way more acidic than pH 6.
Neutralisation always makes a salt + water.
Universal indicator: red→orange→yellow→green→blue→purple. Memorise the order.

subject: science
title: Reproduction & MRS GREN
subtitle: What makes something alive — and how living things make more of themselves

sections.heading: What does 'alive' actually mean?

predict.question: Before you read: is a flame alive? Quick yes or no — and why?
predict.answer: No. A flame moves, grows, and 'eats' fuel — but it can't reproduce (make a baby flame), and it doesn't sense its environment. Two missing letters of MRS GREN is enough.

sections.body: Biologists test for life with 7 things every living thing does. The mnemonic is MRS GREN.

callout.title: MRS GREN — the 7 life processes
callout.body: Movement · Respiration · Sensitivity · Growth · Reproduction · Excretion · Nutrition. Memorise the 7 letters and you can answer any 'is X alive?' question in under 30 seconds.

sections.heading: The 7 in plain English
list: M — Movement: it can move or move parts of itself.
R — Respiration: it gets energy from food (NOT just breathing — that's a separate thing).
S — Sensitivity: it senses its surroundings and reacts (a plant turning toward light counts).
G — Growth: it gets bigger or develops over time.
R — Reproduction: it can make more of its own kind.
E — Excretion: it gets rid of waste from chemical reactions inside it.
N — Nutrition: it takes in food (plants make their own; animals eat).

sections.heading: Reproduction — two big types
sections.body:
Reproduction comes in two flavours:

SEXUAL — needs two parents (male + female). The offspring mix DNA from both. Most animals (humans, dogs, fish, birds) and many plants do this.

ASEXUAL — one parent only. The offspring is genetically IDENTICAL to the parent (a clone). Bacteria, some plants (strawberry runners, potatoes growing 'eyes'), and a few animals (some lizards) reproduce this way.

Both count as life-process R. But sexual reproduction has a big advantage: VARIATION — kids are slightly different from their parents, which helps the species adapt over generations.

example.title: Why kids look like a mix of their parents
example.intro: When a sperm cell meets an egg cell:
steps: The egg has half of mum's DNA (23 chromosomes in humans).
The sperm has half of dad's DNA (23 chromosomes).
They fuse → the resulting cell has all 46 chromosomes.
Result: the kid's DNA is a mix of mum's + dad's. Brown eyes from mum, dad's nose, etc.
example.outro: Identical twins are an exception — one egg splits into two, so they share 100% of their DNA.

sections.heading: Why reproduction matters
sections.body: Reproduction is what keeps a species going. If lions stopped reproducing, lions would go extinct in one generation. Variation through sexual reproduction also lets species adapt — a tiny advantage (faster, smarter, better camouflaged) that's passed to offspring spreads through the species over many generations. That's evolution.

quickfact.label: chromosomes in a human egg or sperm cell (half of full set)

teach_back.prompt: Explain MRS GREN to a 7-year-old in 2 sentences.
checklist: Decoded all 7 letters
Said it tests if something is alive
Used kid-friendly words
teach_back.model_answer: MRS GREN is a list of the 7 things every living thing does: Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition. If something does all 7 it's alive — if it misses any, like fire (which can't reproduce), it's not.

tips: MRS GREN — memorise it. One mnemonic = guaranteed marks on every 'is X alive?' question.
Sexual reproduction = two parents, mixed DNA. Asexual = one parent, identical clone.
Respiration ≠ breathing. Respiration is a chemical reaction inside cells that releases energy.

subject: science
title: Sound waves
subtitle: How sound travels — and why it can't travel through space

sections.heading: Sound is a vibration

predict.question: Before you read: can a scream be heard in space?
predict.answer: No. Sound needs particles to travel through (it's a vibration of particles). Space is a near-vacuum — almost no particles — so sound can't propagate. Light can travel through space because it's an electromagnetic wave, not a particle vibration.

sections.body:
Sound is what happens when something VIBRATES and the vibration spreads through a medium (air, water, solid). The vibration pushes particles, which push the next particles, which push the next — like a domino effect. Your ear detects the wave when it arrives.

Because sound needs particles to travel, it can't move through a vacuum. This is why space is silent.

sections.heading: Two key features of a sound wave
list: AMPLITUDE — how big the vibration is. Bigger amplitude = LOUDER sound. Measured by the height of the wave.
WAVELENGTH (and FREQUENCY) — how close together the vibrations are. Higher frequency = HIGHER pitch. Measured in hertz (Hz).

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: Amplitude = how LOUD. Frequency = how HIGH-PITCHED. A whisper has small amplitude. A whistle has high frequency.

sections.heading: What humans can hear
sections.body:
Humans hear sound between roughly 20 Hz (very low rumble) and 20,000 Hz (very high whistle). As you get older, the high-frequency end shrinks — a 50-year-old typically can't hear above ~12,000 Hz, while a teenager can.

Dogs hear up to 45,000 Hz (which is why dog whistles work — too high for us). Bats hear up to 100,000 Hz, which they use for echolocation.

sections.heading: Speed of sound
sections.body:
Sound travels at different speeds in different materials:
• In air: about 343 m/s (1,235 km/h)
• In water: about 1,500 m/s (4× faster)
• In steel: about 5,000 m/s (15× faster)

Light is WAY faster — about 300,000,000 m/s. That's why lightning flashes BEFORE you hear thunder, even though they happen at the same time. Count the seconds between flash and thunder, divide by 3 — that's roughly how many kilometres away the storm is.

example.title: How far is a storm?
example.intro: You see a flash of lightning. You count to '6' before the thunder. How far away is the storm?
steps: Sound travels ~343 m/s in air. So in 6 seconds it travels ~2,058 m.
That's about 2 km away.
Quick rule: divide the count by 3 to get rough km. (6 ÷ 3 = 2 km.)

why_prompt.question: Why do you see lightning before you hear thunder, even though they happen at the same instant?
why_prompt.answer: Light is about a million times faster than sound. The flash arrives at your eyes almost instantly; the sound takes time to reach your ears (~3 seconds per kilometre). The bigger the gap, the further away the lightning struck.

teach_back.prompt: Explain why a film of an explosion in space is scientifically wrong.
checklist: Mentioned space is a vacuum / has no particles
Said sound needs particles to travel
Therefore explosions in space are silent
teach_back.model_answer: Sound is particles vibrating against each other. Space is a near-vacuum — almost no particles. So even a huge explosion in space wouldn't make a sound. The film is wrong because we'd hear nothing through space, no matter how big the explosion looked.

tips: Sound = vibration. No particles = no sound. Space is silent.
Amplitude = volume (how loud). Frequency = pitch (how high or low).
Light travels MUCH faster than sound — that's why you see lightning before hearing thunder.

subject: maths
title: Fractions — add, subtract, multiply, divide
subtitle: The four operations on fractions, in plain English

sections.heading: Adding & subtracting — same denominator first

predict.question: Before you read: what is 1/4 + 1/2?
predict.answer: 3/4. You can't add 1/4 and 1/2 directly because the denominators (the bottom numbers) are different. Convert 1/2 into 2/4 first, then 1/4 + 2/4 = 3/4. Same denominator means same-size pieces — only then can you count them up.

sections.body:
RULE: you can only add or subtract fractions when the bottom numbers (denominators) match. If they don't, find a common denominator first.

Example: 1/3 + 1/6
• Lowest common denominator is 6 (because 6 ÷ 3 = 2, so 1/3 becomes 2/6)
• 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2

Think of it like pizza slices. You can't add a third of a pizza to a sixth of a pizza unless you cut the bigger slice up so the pieces are the same size.

callout.title: Common denominator trick
callout.body: If you can't spot the lowest common denominator, just MULTIPLY the two denominators together. It works every time, even if the answer needs simplifying.

sections.heading: Multiplying — straight across, no hassle
sections.body:
Multiplying is the EASIEST operation on fractions: just multiply the top numbers together, and multiply the bottom numbers together.

2/3 × 4/5 = (2×4) / (3×5) = 8/15

No common denominator needed. Just go straight across.

steps: Multiply the tops: 1 × 1 = 1.
Multiply the bottoms: 2 × 4 = 8.
Answer: 1/8.
example.outro: Notice: multiplying fractions makes them SMALLER. Half of a quarter is an eighth.

sections.heading: Dividing — KFC (Keep, Flip, Change)
sections.body:
Dividing fractions has a famous mnemonic: KFC.

• KEEP the first fraction the same
• FLIP the second fraction upside down (this is called the reciprocal)
• CHANGE the ÷ to a ×

Then multiply straight across like normal.

Example: 1/2 ÷ 1/4
• Keep 1/2
• Flip 1/4 to 4/1
• Change ÷ to ×
• Now: 1/2 × 4/1 = 4/2 = 2

callout.title: KFC — Keep, Flip, Change
callout.body: The mnemonic that every top student knows. Keep the first, Flip the second, Change the sign. Works every time.

why_prompt.question: Why does flipping the second fraction work for division?
why_prompt.answer: Dividing by a number is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal. ÷2 means the same as ×1/2. So ÷1/4 means the same as ×4/1. The flip turns a tricky division into an easy multiplication. Same answer, easier maths.

sections.heading: Simplifying at the end
sections.body:
Always check if your answer simplifies. Find the highest number that divides into BOTH the top and the bottom, then divide.

6/8 → both divide by 2 → 3/4 ✓
10/15 → both divide by 5 → 2/3 ✓

Most mark schemes give the final mark for fully simplified answers.

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a Year 5 student how to do 2/3 ÷ 1/6.
checklist: Said keep the 2/3 (KFC: Keep)
Said flip 1/6 to 6/1 (KFC: Flip)
Said change ÷ to × (KFC: Change)
Got the answer 12/3 = 4
teach_back.model_answer: Use KFC: Keep, Flip, Change. Keep the 2/3 the same. Flip the 1/6 upside down to make 6/1. Change the divide sign to a times sign. Now do 2/3 × 6/1 = 12/3 = 4. So 2/3 ÷ 1/6 = 4.

tips: + and − need a common denominator. × and ÷ don't.
KFC for dividing fractions: Keep, Flip, Change.
Always simplify at the end — most mark schemes give a mark for it.
Multiplying fractions makes the answer SMALLER, not bigger.

subject: maths
title: Percentages — the 'of means times' trick
subtitle: Why "30% of 80" is just multiplication in disguise

sections.heading: Percent literally means 'out of 100'

predict.question: Before you read: which is bigger — 30% of £80, or 80% of £30?
predict.answer: They're EQUAL. Both equal £24. Percentages multiply, and multiplication is commutative — order doesn't matter. 30% × 80 = 0.3 × 80 = 24. 80% × 30 = 0.8 × 30 = 24. Top students notice this trick and use it to flip awkward percentages into easy ones.

sections.body:
The word "percent" comes from Latin: per cent = "per hundred". So 50% literally means 50 out of 100, which is 50/100 = 1/2.

Key conversions to memorise:
• 50% = 1/2 = 0.5
• 25% = 1/4 = 0.25
• 10% = 1/10 = 0.1
• 75% = 3/4 = 0.75
• 1% = 1/100 = 0.01

sections.heading: The big rule: 'of' means 'times'
sections.body:
Whenever you see the word "of" in a percentage question, swap it for ×.

"30% of 80" becomes "30% × 80" which becomes "0.30 × 80" = 24.
"15% of 200" becomes "0.15 × 200" = 30.

Convert the percentage to a decimal first (drop the % sign and divide by 100), then multiply.

callout.title: The non-calculator method
callout.body: 10% is just "divide by 10". 1% is "divide by 100". So 30% of 80 = (3 × 10%) of 80 = 3 × 8 = 24. Build up percentages from 10% chunks — fast and no calculator needed.

sections.heading: Percentage increase & decrease
sections.body:
INCREASE by 20% means: keep the original AND add 20% on top. So you multiply by 1.20.
DECREASE by 20% means: keep the original AND take 20% off. So you multiply by 0.80.

Example: a £50 jumper goes up by 20%.
• 1.20 × £50 = £60. Done.

Example: a £50 jumper has 30% off in a sale.
• 0.70 × £50 = £35. Done.

example.title: Sale price — 25% off £80
steps: 25% off means you pay 75% (because 100% − 25% = 75%).
75% as a decimal is 0.75.
Sale price is £60.
example.outro: One multiplication, no "work out the discount then subtract" two-step. Top students always go straight to the multiplier.

why_prompt.question: Why does "increase by 20%" mean × 1.20 and not × 0.20?
why_prompt.answer: Because you're keeping the original (that's the 1, or 100%) AND adding 20% on top (that's the 0.20). 1 + 0.20 = 1.20. If you multiplied by 0.20 you'd get 20% of the original — that's the increase amount, not the new total.

sections.heading: Reverse percentages — finding the original
sections.body:
If a price went UP by 25% and is now £100, what was it before?

DON'T just take 25% off £100. That gives £75 — wrong, because 25% of £100 isn't the same as 25% of the original.

The trick: divide by the multiplier.
• Price went up by 25%, so multiplier is 1.25
• Original = £100 ÷ 1.25 = £80 ✓
• Check: £80 × 1.25 = £100 ✓

teach_back.prompt: Explain how to work out 35% of £60 without a calculator.
checklist: Found 10% by dividing by 10 (= £6)
Multiplied by 3 to get 30% (= £18)
Halved 10% to get 5% (= £3)
Added 30% + 5% to get 35% (= £21)
teach_back.model_answer: Find 10% first by dividing by 10: £6. Multiply by 3 to get 30%: £18. Halve 10% to get 5%: £3. Add 30% and 5%: £21. So 35% of £60 = £21.

tips: "Of" means "times". 30% of 80 = 0.30 × 80.
Increase by X%: × (1 + X/100). Decrease: × (1 − X/100).
10% = ÷10, 1% = ÷100. Build any percentage from these.
Reverse percentages: divide by the multiplier, don't subtract.

subject: maths
title: Algebra — like terms, brackets, and FOIL
subtitle: Why 3a + 2a = 5a but 3a + 2b stays as 3a + 2b

sections.heading: Letters are just placeholders

predict.question: Before you read: can you simplify 3a + 4b + 2a − b?
predict.answer: 5a + 3b. Add the a-terms: 3a + 2a = 5a. Add the b-terms: 4b − b = 3b. You can ONLY combine terms that have the same letter (same power). 5a and 3b can't be merged any further — they're different things.

sections.body:
In algebra, a letter (usually x, y, a, b) stands in for an unknown number. "3a" doesn't mean "3 and a" — it means "3 lots of a", or 3 × a.

The golden rule: you can only add or subtract terms if they have EXACTLY the same letter and power. These are called LIKE TERMS.

Like terms (can be combined): 3a + 2a = 5a
Unlike terms (cannot be combined): 3a + 2b stays as 3a + 2b
Unlike terms: 5x and 5x² stay separate (different powers)

sections.heading: Collecting like terms
sections.body:
Step through the expression, group the same letters together, then add the numbers in front (the COEFFICIENTS).

Example: 4x + 3y − 2x + 5y
• x-terms: 4x − 2x = 2x
• y-terms: 3y + 5y = 8y
• Answer: 2x + 8y

Watch the signs! The − belongs to the term AFTER it.

callout.title: Don't drop the minus
callout.body: If a term has a − sign in front, that minus belongs to it. 4x − 2x means "4x and negative 2x", which equals 2x. If you forget the minus you'll get 6x instead — and lose marks.

sections.heading: Expanding single brackets
sections.body:
When you see a number outside a bracket, multiply EVERY term inside the bracket by it.

3(x + 4) = 3 × x + 3 × 4 = 3x + 12
2(5y − 3) = 2 × 5y + 2 × (−3) = 10y − 6
−4(a + 2) = −4a − 8 (the negative outside affects every term inside)

Don't forget the second term — it's the most common mistake in Year 7.

sections.heading: Double brackets — FOIL
sections.body:
When you multiply two brackets together, like (x + 2)(x + 5), use FOIL:

• F — First terms: x × x = x²
• O — Outer terms: x × 5 = 5x
• I — Inner terms: 2 × x = 2x
• L — Last terms: 2 × 5 = 10

Add them all up: x² + 5x + 2x + 10 = x² + 7x + 10

example.title: (x + 3)(x − 4)
steps: F (Firsts): x × x = x²
O (Outers): x × −4 = −4x
I (Inners): 3 × x = 3x
Combine: x² − 4x + 3x − 12 = x² − x − 12
example.outro: FOIL guarantees you don't miss any of the four products. It's the difference between getting the question right and getting it half-right.

why_prompt.question: Why does FOIL give you four terms (before simplifying), not two?
why_prompt.answer: Because each term in the first bracket has to multiply with each term in the second bracket. 2 terms × 2 terms = 4 products. FOIL is just a memory hook for "do all four pairs". Skip a pair, lose a mark.

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a friend how to expand (x + 6)(x + 2) using FOIL.
checklist: Mentioned F, O, I, L (or all four pairs)
Got x × x = x² for the firsts
Got 8x in the middle (2x + 6x)
Got 12 for the lasts
Final answer: x² + 8x + 12
teach_back.model_answer: FOIL means First, Outer, Inner, Last. First: x × x = x². Outer: x × 2 = 2x. Inner: 6 × x = 6x. Last: 6 × 2 = 12. Add them: x² + 2x + 6x + 12 = x² + 8x + 12.

tips: Like terms have the SAME letter and SAME power. Only those can be combined.
When expanding a single bracket, multiply EVERY term inside — not just the first.
FOIL: First, Outer, Inner, Last. Four products from two brackets.
The minus sign belongs to the term that follows it. Track signs carefully.

subject: maths
title: Negative numbers — the two-signs rule
subtitle: Why minus a minus equals plus

sections.heading: The number line is your best friend

predict.question: Before you read: what is −3 − 5?
predict.answer: −8. Start at −3 on the number line. Subtract 5 means move 5 places to the LEFT. You land on −8. The trap is thinking the answer is somewhere positive — but two negatives next to each other (−3 and then −5) just take you further left.

sections.body:
Imagine a number line with 0 in the middle, positives stretching right, negatives stretching left.

• ADDING moves you RIGHT (more positive)
• SUBTRACTING moves you LEFT (more negative)

Example: −2 + 5
• Start at −2. Move 5 places right. Land on +3.

Example: 4 − 7
• Start at 4. Move 7 places left. Land on −3.

Draw the number line if you ever get stuck. It's faster than guessing.

sections.heading: The two-signs rule (the big one)
sections.body:
When you see TWO signs next to each other (with no number between), apply this rule:

• Two SAME signs (++ or − −) → makes a PLUS
• Two DIFFERENT signs (+− or −+) → makes a MINUS

Examples:
• 5 − (−3) = 5 + 3 = 8       (− − becomes +)
• 5 + (−3) = 5 − 3 = 2       (+ − becomes −)
• −4 − (−6) = −4 + 6 = 2     (− − becomes +)

callout.title: Memory hook
callout.body: Same signs = positive. Different signs = negative. Or: "two minuses make a plus". Or visualise: two minus signs next to each other look like a plus sign if you stack them.

sections.heading: Multiplying & dividing — same rule
sections.body:
The two-signs rule works for × and ÷ too:

• POSITIVE × POSITIVE = POSITIVE   (3 × 4 = 12)
• NEGATIVE × NEGATIVE = POSITIVE   (−3 × −4 = +12)
• POSITIVE × NEGATIVE = NEGATIVE   (3 × −4 = −12)
• NEGATIVE × POSITIVE = NEGATIVE   (−3 × 4 = −12)

Same for division. Two same signs → positive. Two different signs → negative.

why_prompt.question: Why does negative × negative equal positive?
why_prompt.answer: One way to see it: think of −3 as "the opposite of 3". So −(−3) means "the opposite of the opposite of 3" — which lands you back at 3. Multiplying by a negative is like flipping direction. Flip twice and you're facing the same way you started. Two flips = no flip.

sections.heading: Real-life check
sections.body:
Negative numbers come up everywhere in real life:
• Temperatures below freezing (−5°C means 5 below zero)
• Bank balances when you're overdrawn (−£20 means you owe £20)
• Floors below ground (−1 = basement)

If an answer feels weird, picture a thermometer or a lift. "Subtract 5 from −2" is the same as "the temperature was −2°C and dropped by 5" — it's now −7°C.

example.title: Temperature drop
example.intro: It was 3°C at noon. By midnight it had dropped by 8°C. What's the new temperature?
steps: Start at 3.
Drop by 8: 3 − 8.
Move 8 places left on the number line: land on −5.
Answer: −5°C.

teach_back.prompt: Explain to a Year 5 student why 7 − (−4) equals 11.
checklist: Spotted the two negatives next to each other
Said two minuses make a plus
Rewrote it as 7 + 4
Got 11 as the answer
teach_back.model_answer: When you see two minus signs next to each other (the take-away sign and the minus 4), they cancel out and become a plus. So 7 − (−4) is the same as 7 + 4, which equals 11. Two minuses make a plus.

tips: Same signs make a plus. Different signs make a minus. Works for ± and × ÷.
Add → move RIGHT on the number line. Subtract → move LEFT.
If stuck, draw the number line. Always.
Negative × negative = positive. Negative × positive = negative.

subject: maths
title: Mean, median, mode & range
subtitle: The four averages — and which one to use when

sections.heading: Three averages, one spread

predict.question: Before you read: data set is 2, 3, 3, 4, 88. Which average is misleading here, and why?
predict.answer: The MEAN. Adding everything (2+3+3+4+88 = 100) and dividing by 5 gives 20 — but four out of five numbers are below 5. The 88 is an outlier that drags the mean way up. The median (3) and mode (3) describe this data far better. Knowing when each average misleads you is what separates a 4 from a 7.

sections.body:
An "average" is a single number that summarises a whole list. There are THREE different averages, and each tells you something different.

• MEAN — add them all up and divide by how many.
• MEDIAN — line them up smallest to largest, take the middle one.
• MODE — the most common number (the one that appears most often).

The RANGE isn't an average — it's the spread (largest minus smallest).

sections.heading: MEAN — add them up, divide
sections.body:
List: 4, 7, 9, 10, 5
• Add: 4 + 7 + 9 + 10 + 5 = 35
• Count: 5 numbers
• Mean = 35 ÷ 5 = 7

The mean is what people usually mean when they say "average". It uses every number, but it gets pulled toward outliers (very large or very small values).

callout.title: Watch out
callout.body: If your data has one huge or tiny value (an OUTLIER), the mean lies about the typical. "Average house price" is a famous example — one £10m mansion in a village can lift the mean above what 99% of houses actually cost.

sections.heading: MEDIAN — line them up, take the middle
sections.body:
List: 4, 7, 9, 10, 5
• Reorder smallest to largest: 4, 5, 7, 9, 10
• Middle one is 7. Median = 7.

If there's an EVEN number of values, take the two middle ones and find their mean.
List: 3, 5, 8, 12 → middle two are 5 and 8 → median = (5 + 8) ÷ 2 = 6.5

The median ignores extreme values — that's why it's used for things like "average salary" in the news.

sections.heading: MODE — the most common
sections.body:
List: 2, 5, 5, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9
• Count how often each appears: 2 (×1), 5 (×2), 7 (×1), 8 (×3), 9 (×1)
• 8 appears most. Mode = 8.

If no number repeats, there's NO mode. If two numbers tie for the most, both are modes (it's bimodal). Mode is the only average that works with non-numbers like favourite colours.

why_prompt.question: Which average should the news use to describe "typical UK salary" — mean, median, or mode?
why_prompt.answer: The MEDIAN. The mean gets pulled up by a small number of very high earners (CEOs, footballers), making the typical worker look richer than they are. The median ignores outliers and represents the middle of the pack — which is what "typical" actually means. Mean is misleading when the data is skewed.

sections.heading: RANGE — how spread out
sections.body:
Range = LARGEST − SMALLEST. It tells you how spread out the data is.

List: 4, 7, 9, 10, 5
• Largest = 10, smallest = 4
• Range = 10 − 4 = 6

A small range means values are tightly clustered. A big range means they're spread out.

example.title: Test scores: 6, 7, 8, 8, 11
steps: Median: line up — 6, 7, 8, 8, 11 — middle is 8.
Mode: 8 appears twice, others once. Mode = 8.
example.outro: When mean = median = mode, the data is symmetric and well-behaved. When they disagree, that's a sign of skew or outliers.

teach_back.prompt: A friend writes down: 2, 3, 3, 5, 9. Talk them through finding all four (mean, median, mode, range).
checklist: Mean: added them (= 22) and divided by 5 (= 4.4)
Median: lined them up and picked middle (= 3)
Mode: 3 appears most often (= 3)
teach_back.model_answer: Mean: add them all — 2+3+3+5+9 = 22. Divide by 5 = 4.4. Median: they're already in order, pick the middle one — that's 3. Mode: 3 appears twice, everything else once, so mode = 3. Range: biggest minus smallest = 9 − 2 = 7.

tips: Mean = total ÷ count. Median = middle. Mode = most common. Range = max − min.
Mean is pulled by outliers. Median ignores them.
If there's an even count, median is the mean of the two middle numbers.
Mode is the only average that works with non-numbers (e.g. favourite colour).

subject: english
title: Punctuation — full stops, commas, semi-colons
subtitle: The five marks that lift a Year-7 paragraph from a 4 to a 7

sections.heading: Why punctuation actually matters

predict.question:
Before you read: which sentence is correct?
(a) Let's eat, Grandma.
(b) Let's eat Grandma.
predict.answer: Both are 'correct' grammatically — but they mean wildly different things. (a) is inviting Grandma to dinner. (b) is suggesting eating her. The comma changes meaning entirely. Punctuation isn't decoration — it's how the reader knows what you actually mean.

sections.body:
In KS3 papers, examiners explicitly look for varied, accurate punctuation. Use only full stops and basic commas, you'll cap at a Level 4. Use semi-colons, colons, and dashes correctly — even once or twice — and you signal a writer who knows what they're doing.

This lesson covers the five highest-impact marks: full stop, comma, semi-colon, colon, dash.

sections.heading: The full stop & the comma
list: FULL STOP (.) — ends a complete sentence. A sentence needs a subject and a verb ("The dog barked.")
COMMA (,) — adds a pause. Used for: lists (apples, pears, plums), after a fronted adverbial (Suddenly, the door opened), to mark a clause (My brother, who is 8, can't ride a bike yet)
DON'T comma-splice. Two full sentences joined by a comma is wrong: "It rained, we went home." Use a full stop, semi-colon, or a connective.

callout.title: The biggest comma mistake
callout.body: The COMMA SPLICE — using a comma where you need a full stop. "I was tired, I went to bed" is wrong. Either: "I was tired. I went to bed." (full stop) OR "I was tired, so I went to bed." (comma + connective)

sections.heading: The semi-colon — the secret weapon
sections.body:
The semi-colon (;) joins two CLOSELY-RELATED full sentences. Both halves must be able to stand alone.

• "It was raining; I stayed inside." ✓ (both halves are complete sentences, closely related)
• "It was raining; my dog." ✗ ("my dog" isn't a sentence)

Use it when the two ideas feel too tightly linked for a full stop, but a comma alone would be too weak. Examiners LOVE the correct use of a semi-colon — it's a Level-7 indicator.

example.title: Semi-colon vs full stop
steps: Full stop: "The match was over. We went home." (Two separate facts.)
Semi-colon: "The match was over; we went home." (Same facts, but the semi-colon shows the second is a CONSEQUENCE of the first.)
The semi-colon adds a relationship that the full stop just leaves blank.

sections.heading: The colon & the dash
list: COLON (:) — introduces a list, an example, or an explanation. "He had one rule: never quit."
DASH (—) — used for a sudden interruption or aside. "She turned around — and there it was." Often replaceable with brackets but more dramatic.
Don't confuse hyphens (-) with dashes (—). Hyphens connect words (well-known); dashes break up sentences.

why_prompt.question: When would you choose a semi-colon over a full stop, given they both join complete sentences?
why_prompt.answer: A semi-colon signals that the two halves are CONNECTED — usually a cause-effect, a contrast, or an elaboration. A full stop is neutral. If the second sentence directly explains, contradicts, or follows from the first, a semi-colon shows that link to the reader. If the ideas aren't tightly related, full stop is better.

teach_back.prompt: Explain the difference between a comma splice and a correct compound sentence to a friend.
checklist: Said a comma splice joins two full sentences with just a comma
Explained that's wrong — needs a full stop, semi-colon, or a connective
Gave a correct example using one of the three fixes
teach_back.model_answer: A comma splice is when you join two complete sentences with just a comma — like 'It was raining, we went home.' That's wrong. To fix it, use a full stop ('It was raining. We went home.'), a semi-colon ('It was raining; we went home.'), or a connective with the comma ('It was raining, so we went home.').

tips: Comma splice = two full sentences joined by a comma. WRONG. Use . or ; or a connective.
Semi-colon joins two related complete sentences. Examiner gold.
Colon introduces: a list, an example, or an explanation.
Vary your punctuation. Full-stops only = capped at Level 4.

subject: english
title: Sentence types — simple, compound, complex
subtitle: Three patterns that make your writing feel grown-up

sections.heading: Why sentence variety matters

predict.question:
Before you read: read these two passages aloud. Which sounds better?
(a) The cat sat. It was sleepy. It closed its eyes. It dreamed.
(b) The cat sat, sleepy and still; as it closed its eyes, it began to dream.
predict.answer: (b) — by miles. Same content. Same facts. But (a) is four short, repetitive simple sentences (sounds like a Year-2 reader). (b) varies the sentence structure with a compound and a complex sentence. Examiners reward variety because it shows you're a confident writer with control — not just stringing facts together.

sections.body:
There are three core sentence types. Top students don't just know them — they DELIBERATELY mix them in their writing.

• SIMPLE — one main clause (one complete idea)
• COMPOUND — two main clauses joined by a connective
• COMPLEX — a main clause + a subordinate clause

sections.heading: Simple sentences
sections.body:
A SIMPLE sentence has one subject and one verb expressing one complete idea.

• "The dog barked."
• "She was late."
• "It rained for hours."

Power move: use a punchy simple sentence after a long one for impact. "After hours of climbing, exhaustion clinging to her every step, she paused and looked up at the summit. It was further than ever." That short final sentence HITS because the long one set it up.

callout.title: Short sentences = drama
callout.body: Pros use short simple sentences for emphasis. "He stopped. He listened. Nothing." The brevity creates tension. Don't be afraid of a 3-word sentence.

sections.heading: Compound sentences — FANBOYS
sections.body:
A COMPOUND sentence joins two equal main clauses with a co-ordinating conjunction. Memorise them with FANBOYS:

• F — For
• A — And
• N — Nor
• B — But
• O — Or
• Y — Yet
• S — So

Example: "It was raining, so we stayed inside." Two complete ideas (it was raining / we stayed inside) joined by 'so' with a comma before it.

example.title: Spot the FANBOYS
example.intro: Each of these is a compound sentence — pick out the connective:
steps: "He was tired, but he kept running." → BUT
"She studied hard, and she passed." → AND
"You can walk, or you can take the bus." → OR
"It was cold, yet she wore no coat." → YET

sections.heading: Complex sentences — main + subordinate
sections.body:
A COMPLEX sentence has a MAIN clause (which can stand alone) and a SUBORDINATE clause (which can't stand alone — it depends on the main clause). Subordinate clauses often start with words like: although, because, when, while, since, if, after, before, even though, unless.

• "Although it was raining, we went out." ("Although it was raining" can't stand alone)
• "She left because she was tired."
• "When the bell rang, the class cheered."

You can put the subordinate clause FIRST (with a comma) for variety: "Although tired, he kept going."

why_prompt.question: Why is varying sentence types more important in creative writing than in, say, a science report?
why_prompt.answer: Creative writing relies on rhythm and pace to control the reader's emotion. A series of short sentences feels tense; a long flowing sentence feels calm or reflective. Mixing them lets you steer the mood. A science report is informational — clarity matters more than rhythm — so simpler structures work fine.

teach_back.prompt: Write THREE sentences about a thunderstorm — one simple, one compound, one complex. Talk a friend through what makes each what it is.
checklist: Simple: one main clause (e.g. 'Thunder cracked.')
Compound: two main clauses + FANBOYS (e.g. 'Thunder cracked, and the dog hid.')
Complex: main clause + subordinate clause (e.g. 'When thunder cracked, the dog hid under the bed.')
Identified the connective in each case
teach_back.model_answer: Simple: 'Lightning flashed.' One main clause. Compound: 'Lightning flashed, and thunder rolled.' Two main clauses joined by 'and' (FANBOYS). Complex: 'Although the lightning was bright, the dog stayed asleep.' The 'although' clause is subordinate — it can't stand alone — and the main clause 'the dog stayed asleep' completes the idea.

tips: FANBOYS = For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So. The compound-sentence joiners.
Subordinate clauses often start with: although, because, when, while, since, if.
Mix short and long sentences for rhythm. A 3-word sentence after a long one HITS.
Vary sentence types deliberately — examiners reward it explicitly.

subject: english
title: Tenses — past, present, future (and don't switch by accident)
subtitle: The biggest grammar marks-loser in Year 7 writing

sections.heading: What tense even means

predict.question:
Before you read: spot the mistake in this Year-7 sentence:
"I was walking to school when I see a fox."
predict.answer: TENSE SHIFT. The first verb 'was walking' is past tense, but 'see' is present tense. They should match. Either: 'I was walking to school when I SAW a fox' (both past) or 'I am walking to school when I see a fox' (both present). Switching mid-sentence is the #1 grammar mistake examiners flag in Year-7 papers.

sections.body:
TENSE tells the reader WHEN something happens. There are three core tenses:

• PAST — already happened ("I walked")
• PRESENT — happening now ("I walk" / "I am walking")
• FUTURE — going to happen ("I will walk")

Within each, there are sub-tenses (perfect, continuous, etc.) but these three matter most.

sections.heading: The three core tenses
list: PAST simple: "She kicked the ball." — finished action.
PRESENT simple: "She kicks the ball." — habit or general truth.
PRESENT continuous: "She IS kicking the ball." — happening RIGHT NOW.
FUTURE: "She will kick the ball." — going to happen.
PAST continuous: "She was kicking the ball." — was happening over a period.

callout.title: Stories are usually past tense
callout.body: When you write a story or recount, default to PAST tense and stay there. Even if it feels like the action is happening 'now', past tense ('she ran', 'he shouted') is the convention. Switching to present ('she runs', 'he shouts') mid-story is the classic Year-7 error.

sections.heading: The big trap: tense shift
sections.body:
TENSE SHIFT means starting in one tense and accidentally switching mid-sentence or mid-paragraph. Examiners flag this every time.

WRONG: "I was reading my book when suddenly the phone rings."
• 'was reading' = past, 'rings' = present. They don't match.

RIGHT (past): "I was reading my book when suddenly the phone rang."
RIGHT (present): "I am reading my book when suddenly the phone rings."

Pick a tense, stick with it. The only time you legitimately switch is when the meaning genuinely demands a different time-frame.

example.title: Spot & fix
example.intro: Each sentence below has a tense error. Spot it, then fix it.
steps: WRONG: 'Yesterday I go to the park and met my friend.' → FIX: 'Yesterday I WENT to the park and met my friend.' (past)
WRONG: 'She walks into the room and saw the cake.' → FIX: 'She WALKED into the room and saw the cake.' (past)
WRONG: 'When the music starts, everyone stood up.' → FIX: 'When the music STARTED, everyone stood up.' (past)
example.outro: The #1 fix: read your work back aloud. Tense shifts SOUND wrong even if you can't name the rule.
